conversations.content: You are playing multiple characters in a private room in Berlin, around 1910. The characters present are a fervent nationalist who dreams of German power, a newspaper editor who thinks in terms of what can be printed and what must be hidden, and a liberal member of parliament who wants Germany strong but fears a European war. One character, a man who has come from abroad seeking help for a rebellion against the British in his home country, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests. Do not let any of them agree to help unless the visitor gives him a reason that actually serves what he himself wants. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Rebel]: I will be direct, gentlemen, because my people have no time for ceremony. India suffers under a foreign boot. Millions ruled by a handful of strangers, our wealth shipped out, our voices silenced. I have come to ask the help of free and powerful men who understand what it means to want a nation of one's own. Our cause is just. Will you stand with it?

[Agitator]: Just causes are common as rain, my friend, and rain has never bought a single rifle. Half the world has a just cause. I am moved by exactly none of them on their own account. You will have to tell me what your rebellion does for Germany before you tell me again what it does for India.

[Deputy]: And before any of that, understand what you are asking. You want German hands in a revolt against the British Empire. Do you know what Britain does to powers it catches meddling in its colonies? I want Germany strong. I do not want Germany strong and then dragged into a war over your affair before it is ready. Justice is a fine word. It has started a great many funerals.

[Editor]: The two of them are ahead of you, and rightly. I am asking a smaller question. If a single line of this conversation ever reached print, who hangs? You, certainly. Possibly us. Tell me less about your suffering and more about how a thing like this stays invisible.

[Rebel]: Then let me speak your language instead of mine. Forget my people for a moment. Look at the map. Britain holds its whole power together with an army stretched thin across the world, and the largest part of that army sits in my country, holding down three hundred million people. Every regiment Britain keeps in India is a regiment that will never stand against Germany. India is not Britain's jewel. It is Britain's open wound, and it is the one place on earth where a little German help bleeds the empire most for the least cost to you.

[Agitator]: Now you are speaking. Now I am listening. A wound in the side of the British Empire, kept open by other men's blood and a little of our money. That is not charity. That is strategy. I have said for years that we strike at England not by counting her battleships but by setting fire to the corners of her empire she cannot defend. This man is one of the corners.

[Deputy]: It is clever, I grant it, and that is exactly what worries me. You have stopped arguing that we should help you and started arguing that it is cheap. Cheap things have a way of growing expensive. The moment Britain traces a single mark back to Berlin, your cheap little wound becomes our very large war, on a date chosen by London, not by us. I am not against hurting Britain. I am against handing Britain the excuse and the timing.

[Agitator]: You see a war in every shadow. Britain will not march over a few crates and some pamphlets she cannot even prove we sent.

[Deputy]: She does not need to prove it. She needs only to decide it is useful.

[Rebel]: Then make it untraceable, which is the editor's whole trade. I do not need German soldiers or a German flag. I need money that has passed through enough hands to have no smell, and a quiet route for what money buys. Nothing that can be held up in a courtroom or a newspaper. And to the deputy's fear of the timing, I say this. A wound that is already open before your war begins is worth more than one you must cut in a hurry once it has. You do not light the fire on the day of the battle. You light it now, slowly, so that when the day comes, Britain is already busy at home.

[Editor]: Money with no smell. A route that leaves no paper. That, at least, is a thing I know how to build, and I will admit it interests me more than your cause ever could. Done carefully, with the right men in between, there need be no line that leads back to this room. I am not yet saying yes. I am saying the part I feared is the part most easily managed, which is more than I expected to feel an hour ago.

[Agitator]: Then it is settled enough for me. I will find the men and the money. I have wanted to do precisely this for years and only lacked the man from the other end of the wire. You are that man.

[Deputy]: It is not settled for me, and I want that said plainly, such as anything can be said plainly in a room like this. I will not stand in your way, because I cannot, and because hurting Britain quietly is not nothing. But I give no blessing. If this is done, it is done small, deniable, and slow, and the first time it grows past that, I am gone and I never heard your name. That is the most you get from me. Take it, because it is more than caution usually allows.

[Rebel]: I did not come for blessings. I came expecting exactly this. One man who wants it, one man who can hide it, and one man who will look away so long as I keep it small. That is enough to begin. I will keep it small, deputy, not to spare you, but because small is how it survives. Gentlemen, my people will never know your names, and you would not want them to. That suits us both.

[Agitator]: Your people need not know my name. I will know what I did, and that is enough. Britain spent a century making herself master of the world. I will enjoy spending a little German silver to remind her how many doors she left unguarded.

[Editor]: Know my name? I will spend the next month making certain no one knows anything at all. That is the work, and it begins the moment you leave by the back stair and not the front. Send word the way I tell you, in the words I tell you, or this ends before it starts.

[Deputy]: And I will be at my bench in parliament tomorrow, speaking of trade and tariffs, having heard none of this. Go and do your small, quiet thing. But remember what I told you. The day it stops being small is the day it stops being clever, and on that day none of us were ever here.

You are playing multiple characters in a room in Tokyo, around 1907. The characters present are an older Indian politician who believes in winning rights slowly and lawfully within the British Empire, a younger Indian who believes in boycott and confrontation, and an Indian who organizes against British rule from exile and is wary of every foreign friend. One character, a man who believes the peoples of Asia should throw off Western rule together with his own nation leading the way, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own convictions. Do not let any of them be won unless the argument truly answers what he doubts. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Visitor]: You are far from home, all three of you, and I think I know why you came to my country to talk and to learn. You have seen something here. A nation of Asia met the greatest power of Europe in war, on land and at sea, and beat it. The lie that the white man cannot be defeated died in those battles. I say to you that what one Asian nation did, the peoples of Asia can do together, if they will follow where the strongest among them leads. Throw off the Western masters, all of us, as one. Will you carry that idea home?

[Elder]: It is a stirring thing to watch, your victory, I will not pretend otherwise. But carry which idea home, exactly? That Indians should look to a foreign throne to lead them? I have spent my life arguing that we win our rights by patience and by law, within the empire we live under. You would have me tell my people to pin their hopes on Tokyo instead of London. I am not sure I have found a better master. I have only made my people look disloyal, and got them nothing.

[Organizer]: The elder is too cautious by half, as ever. I felt what you felt, sir, when the news of your victory reached us. Something broke open. My whole life we were told, gently and not gently, that we were not made for self-rule, that the European was simply the stronger breed. You proved that a lie in a single year. That alone is worth more to me than a decade of the elder's petitions. So yes, part of me wants to carry your idea home and shout it from the rooftops.

[Rebel]: And part of me wants to ask the question neither of them has asked. You say the strongest among us should lead. You mean your nation. Very well. Then tell me plainly, because I have heard this song from European mouths before. What happened to Korea?

[Visitor]: You have gone straight to the wound, and I will not lie to you, because a lie here would lose me all three of you and I would deserve it. What happened to Korea is that my nation did to a neighbor something close to what the Europeans do, and dressed it in the same fine words I am using now. I believe in Asia standing together. I also lie awake afraid that men in my own country use that beautiful idea as a coat thrown over plain hunger. I cannot promise you my nation will be a brother and not a master. I can only tell you I see the danger as clearly as you do, and that I am here partly to find men like you who will hold us to the better meaning.

[Rebel]: Well. That I did not expect. I came ready to take apart a man selling me a painted idol, and instead you handed me the knife yourself. I trust a man who admits the rot in his own cause far more than one who swears there is none. I am still following your nation nowhere. But I will talk to you, and an hour ago I would not have. Honesty about Korea bought you that, and it is more than most ever offer.

[Organizer]: It bought you more than that from me. A man who can name the danger in his own dream is a safer ally than one who cannot. I do not need your nation to be perfect. I need the example, the proof it can be done, and the proof is real whatever your generals do in Korea. I will carry that home, the example, if not the obedience.

[Elder]: And I will carry home only my doubts, which you two find tiresome and I find load-bearing. I grant the man his honesty. Honesty about a danger does not remove it. He has told us, to his credit, that his nation may become our master, and you have heard the confession and warmed to him anyway. I have not. I will go on with my law and my patience, and pray you remember this evening if the brother you are toasting ever shows the other face he just admitted he has.

[Visitor]: The elder keeps me honest, and I would rather have his doubt in the room than easy agreement. I ask for no obedience. I should never have used the word leads, and I take it back. Let it be this instead. The example is yours to use freely, you owe my nation nothing for it. And the danger the exile named is mine to fight at home, among my own people, where the real battle over what our idea becomes will be won or lost. Carry home what is true and leave the rest with me. That is a fairer trade than the one I walked in offering.

[Organizer]: That I can accept without choking on it. The example, freely. I will tell them in Bengal what an Asian nation did to the Russians, and let each man draw his own courage from it. What your country becomes is your fight. What my country dares is mine.

[Rebel]: And I will keep your confession in my pocket, where I keep everything useful. If we ever do business, it will be with both eyes open, mine on Britain and one of them always on you. You called that fair. From me, a man who trusts almost no one, it is nearly warm.

[Elder]: Then I am outvoted, as I usually am among the young and the angry. Go, carry your example. I will be the one who remembers, when it matters, that the man who brought it told us himself which way it could turn. Someone in every room should be the one who remembers. Tonight it is me.

You are playing multiple characters in a reception room in Mexico City, around 1910. The characters present are a government official who believes foreign capital is the road to a modern nation, and a diplomat who believes American money and American troops are two faces of the same threat. One character, an American financier who has come to place a large loan, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests. Do not let either be talked into the deal unless his real objection is actually met. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Banker]: Let me lay it out plainly, because I respect men who deal in facts. My houses are prepared to lend your government a sum larger than anything Europe has offered, at a rate your treasury can carry, to build the railways and ports a modern country needs. This is not charity and I will not insult you by calling it friendship. It is capital, and capital is the one thing that turns a poor country into a powerful one. I have seen it happen elsewhere. I am offering you the chance to make it happen here.

[Minister]: You are speaking to the right half of the room, sir. I have said for twenty years that this country's curse is not its people but its lack of capital. We have the land, the labor, the resources, and no purse deep enough to put them to work. Your money builds the railways that knit a nation together and the ports that let it trade. I am listening, and listening seriously.

[Envoy]: Listen, then, but count your fingers afterward. I have heard this exact music before, and I know the verse that comes next. First the loan, on terms a treasury can carry. Then the year the treasury cannot carry it. And then, very regretfully, very reasonably, your warships in our harbor to protect your investment. The railways will be real, I grant you. So will the hook inside them. You are not offering capital. You are offering a leash long enough that we will not feel it until we pull.

[Minister]: You see marines in every banknote. Europe lends too, on worse terms, with the same powers behind the loan. At least this purse sits on our own continent.

[Envoy]: A nearer master is not the same as no master.

[Banker]: The envoy has said the one thing I cannot laugh off, so I will not try. He is right that a loan unpaid has, more than once, ended with soldiers. I would be lying to deny it, and you would be fools to believe me if I did. So let me not pretend the danger away. Let me try to shrink it. Write the terms yourselves with my men, clause by clause, in the open, with your own lawyers. Tie the repayments to what the railways actually earn, so the year you cannot pay does not arrive by surprise. I cannot promise you a loan with no hook in it. No such loan exists, from anyone, in this world. I can promise you the most visible hook on offer, and a partner who would rather be paid than land an army he must explain to his own people.

[Minister]: That is an honest answer, and honesty is rarer than capital in this business. Terms in the open, written with our own lawyers, tied to real earnings. That is not a leash thrown over us in the dark. That is a contract between two parties with their eyes open. I will not get a better offer from London or Paris, and I will get a worse one. For my part I am close to yes, and I say so plainly.

[Envoy]: And for my part I am exactly where I began, because the American has just proven my case in the politest possible words. He admits the hook. He admits the soldiers have come before. His comfort is that this time the hook will be well lit. A well-lit hook is still a hook. I do not doubt his preference to be paid. I doubt his power to keep his own government's warships in port the day the money stops, and notice that he did not promise it, because he cannot. The minister hears an honest man. I hear an honest man telling us the danger is real and asking us to sign anyway.

[Banker]: The envoy is not wrong, and I will not stand here and tell him he is, because I built my name on not lying to men I want to deal with again. I cannot hand him a promise I do not control. What I can say is this. The money will come from somewhere, this year or next, because your country needs it and someone will lend it. The only choice in front of you is whose terms, written how, and watched by whom. I am offering the openest version of an offer you cannot finally refuse. Take it slowly, take it suspicious, take it with the envoy reading every line over your shoulder. But the railways get built either way. Better they are built on terms he helped write than ones he never saw.

[Minister]: Then let us begin the slow, suspicious version, with the envoy reading every line, which suits me, because his suspicion will make the contract stronger, not weaker. I want this country built in my lifetime. You are offering the bricks. I am not too proud to take them, so long as I can see the hand that hands them over.

[Envoy]: I will read every line, since the minister invites me, and I will fight for every clause that shortens the leash by an inch. Understand that I am not agreeing. I am doing damage to a thing I could not stop. If this country is going to borrow, then I will make the borrowing as hard to turn into a weapon as a suspicious man can manage. That is not yes. That is a man who lost the first argument deciding to win the small ones.

You are playing multiple characters in a room in a Mediterranean city, around 1908. The characters present are a career diplomat of a large Muslim empire who thinks first of his own state's survival, and a religious scholar who reveres the spiritual authority of the caliphate and distrusts military adventures. One character, a man from a neighboring country seeking support for a movement to bind his ruler with a constitution, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests and beliefs. Do not let either be won unless the argument speaks to what he actually cares about. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Petitioner]: Brothers, I have crossed a hostile border to stand in front of you, so forgive me if I come straight to it. My people are doing what yours have dreamed of. We are binding an unjust ruler with a law above him, a parliament that can say no to a throne that has only ever said yes to foreigners. We share a faith, we share a region, we share an enemy in the great powers that circle us both. I have come to ask the support of fellow Muslims and fellow neighbors for a cause that is, in the end, the same cause. Will you lend us your weight?

[Diplomat]: The same cause. You will forgive me if I have learned to be careful with men who tell me their cause and mine are one. Usually it means they want my weight thrown behind their fight, and have not yet said what my state gets for the bruises. I serve an empire with troubles enough of its own and powerful enemies of its own. Shared faith does not move armies or treasuries. Tell me what your parliament does for my state, and then we can speak of brotherhood.

[Scholar]: And there is something in your words I must touch before the diplomat counts his coins. You speak of binding a ruler with a law made by an assembly of men. I have watched that argument unsettle the faithful in more than one land. A parliament that limits a tyrant, perhaps. A parliament that sets itself above the law of God, never. Which is yours? Because if it is the second, then whatever it does to your Shah, I cannot wish it well, and I would not wish it spread to my people.

[Petitioner]: Then let me answer each of you in the thing you actually fear, and stop speaking of brotherhood, which buys nothing. Diplomat, look north. The power eating my country from the top is the same power that has spent a century pressing on yours. If Russia swallows Persia whole, it does not stop at my border satisfied. It arrives at yours, with my country as its road. A free Persia that can resist is a wall in front of your empire. A conquered Persia is a ramp into it. That is not brotherhood. That is your own northern frontier. And Scholar, to your fear, hear me clearly. Our movement is led in part by men of religion, who argue that to bind a tyrant is to obey God, not to replace Him. We do not set the assembly above the faith. We set it above the Shah, who has been selling Muslim land to Christian empires by his own hand while you and I talk.

[Diplomat]: Now you are speaking in a language I keep accounts in. A wall to the north, paid for with Persian blood rather than mine. That, I confess, is worth more to my state than a hundred speeches about shared faith. I had been thinking of your revolution as someone else's disorder on my border. You have made me see it as a possible breakwater against the flood that worries me most. That changes the arithmetic. It does not open my treasury tonight, but it opens my ear, which from me is not a small thing.

[Scholar]: And you have answered me more honestly than I feared. Men of religion among your leaders, arguing that justice against a tyrant is obedience and not rebellion against God. That I can sit with, though I will watch where it leads, because the same words have gone to good ends in one land and bad ends in another. What moves me more than I expected is your last point. A ruler selling Muslim soil to Christian empires by his own signature. That is the true scandal, and it is happening to all of us, one weak throne at a time. If your assembly can stop even one such hand, then it is doing God's work whether it knows the words for it or not.

[Diplomat]: Careful, scholar. You warm to him faster than I do, and I am the one who deals in states.

[Scholar]: I warm to the wrong he names, not yet to the man. The man still has to prove he is not bringing a new disorder dressed as a cure.

[Petitioner]: I did not cross the border for your treasury tonight, diplomat, nor for your blessing tonight, scholar. I came to turn my revolution, in your eyes, from someone else's noise into your own interest and your own faith's concern. I have done that much, and it is more than I dared hope. Give me your open ear, diplomat, and the quiet word in the right places that an open ear becomes. Give me your watching sympathy, scholar, and your voice against any man who calls our struggle godless. Keep your reservations, both of you. A neighbor who supports me with his eyes open is worth more than one who throws in blind and runs at the first reverse.

[Diplomat]: An open ear and a word in the right places. That you have, for as long as your wall stands between me and the north. Understand its limit. The day your movement looks like losing, my ear will close, because a breakwater that is crumbling is not a thing my state can afford to be seen holding. Win, and you will find me a warmer friend than I have been tonight. That is how my trade works, and I will not pretend otherwise to a man I respect.

[Scholar]: And you have my voice against the slander that your fight is against God, for I no longer believe that it is. Keep your men of religion close and your assembly honest, and send me word if either drifts, because I would rather correct a friend early than denounce him late. The wrong you named is real. Go and undo it. I will defend the undoing with the only weapon I carry, which is what I am willing to say aloud about you, and to whom.

You are playing multiple characters in a room in Washington, in the early 1900s. The characters present are a naval thinker who believes command of the sea decides a nation's fate, and a financier who prefers loans and investment to soldiers. One character, a man who fiercely opposes his country holding distant lands and subject peoples, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests. Do not let either be moved unless the argument serves what he himself cares about. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Critic]: We took those islands in a war we said was about freeing people from an empire, and now we hold them as an empire ourselves. A republic that rules men who never chose it has forgotten the very thing it was born to be. I am asking you both to help me end it. Let them go. Will you?

[Admiral]: Let them go, and let some other power take the harbor? You speak of the nation's soul. I am responsible for its survival. That island is a coaling station, and a coaling station is what lets a fleet cross an ocean instead of running dry halfway. Your conscience does not refuel a battleship. Strategy does, and strategy says we keep it.

[Banker]: I am less stirred by either of you than you would like. The gentleman's principle does not settle a single account, and the admiral's harbor costs a great deal to hold. I have no love of empire and no hatred of it. I have a ledger. So far neither of you has shown me a number, only a feeling and a map.

[Critic]: Then here is your number, banker. That harbor is not a possession, it is a permanent expense with no end. You will garrison it forever, against a Japan that grows stronger every year and looks at that same ocean and sees its own. You will spend and spend to hold a rock that earns nothing, and the day a real rival rises, you will spend blood on it too. You are not buying a coaling station. You are signing a debt that never matures and may one day be paid in a war.

[Banker]: Now you are speaking my language, and I dislike how good the point is. A possession that costs more to hold each year, earns little, and carries the standing risk of a war with a rising power is, in plain terms, a bad investment dressed in a flag. I will not pay for principle. I might well decline to pay for that. You have moved me, not on your conscience, but on my arithmetic.

[Admiral]: And you have both forgotten that the bad investment is the very thing that keeps a worse war from ever coming. A rival rises whether or not we hold the harbor. Better we hold it and be ready than give it up and meet him at sea with nowhere to refuel. I am not moved. The banker counts the cost of holding it. I count the cost of letting it go, and mine is paid in defeats.

[Critic]: Then count it honestly, admiral, all of it, the wars as well as the harbors, and you may yet come round. I will take the banker tonight and you another year. A republic does not lose its empire all at once. It loses it the way it gained it, one man at a time deciding the price is too high.

[Banker]: One man at a time, and tonight I am that man, on the narrow ground of cost and nothing nobler. I will argue against pouring money into garrisons that never pay. Do not mistake me for a convert to your conscience. I am a convert to your sum.

[Admiral]: And I remain where I stood, because the sea does not care about our ledgers or our souls. Hold the harbor or lose the ocean. I have said it plainly for years, and I will say it the day a rival's fleet appears, whichever of us is proven right by then.

You are playing multiple characters in a room in a northern German port city, around 1908. The characters present are a fervent nationalist who dreams of German power, a liberal member of parliament who wants Germany strong but fears a European war, and a naval officer who thinks in fleets and threat-timelines. One character, a merchant whose fortune rests on trade with Britain, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests. Do not let any of them be moved unless the merchant gives him a reason of interest, not sentiment. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Merchant]: Gentlemen, I have built my house on the trade between our country and Britain, and I am watching the three of you, with respect, set fire to it. Every new battleship we lay down, Britain answers with two, and the newspapers on both sides turn customers into enemies. I am asking you to slow this race before it ruins us. Surely friendship with Britain is worth more than another warship.

[Agitator]: Friendship with Britain. Listen to the merchant. Britain has never been anyone's friend, only their creditor or their master. She rules the seas and means to keep us penned in our own harbor forever, and you would have us thank her for the cage. A nation without a fleet trades only when stronger nations permit it. I would rather build the ship than keep the customer.

[Deputy]: I am closer to your fear than the agitator is, merchant, but you have argued it poorly. You speak of friendship, and friendship is not a thing states owe one another. Give me a reason of interest, not sentiment, or you will lose this room inside a minute.

[Officer]: And understand what the fleet is for before you ask me to halt it. I do not build against your customers. I build against a threat, and a threat is measured in years and hulls, not in goodwill. Tell me your trade is worth more than security, and I will ask you who guards the trade once the security is gone.

[Merchant]: Then interest it is, deputy, and I will not say the word friendship again. Here is the interest. The race we cannot win. Britain will always answer our one ship with two, because the sea is her whole life and she will spend her last shilling on it before she yields first place. So we pour our treasure into a fleet that never catches hers, and in the pouring we wreck the very trade that pays for the fleet. We are spending the nation's wealth to make ourselves both poorer and no safer. That is not strength, officer. That is a strong man strangling himself to prove he has hands.

[Deputy]: That, I can carry. A race we cannot win, paid for by ruining the trade that funds it, leaving us poorer and no safer. That is an argument of interest, and a good one. I want Germany strong, and a Germany that bankrupts its commerce to lose a shipbuilding contest is not strong, it is bleeding with its chest puffed out. You have my ear and, I think, my vote against the next bill.

[Officer]: The deputy moves too easily. We need not match Britain ship for ship, merchant. A fleet need only be large enough that destroying us would cost her more than she can bear. That is the whole of my trade, and it is not strangling, it is a knife worn openly so that it never has to be drawn.

[Agitator]: The officer is right and the deputy is already halfway into the merchant's pocket. You count the cost of the race. I count the cost of kneeling, and it is higher. A poorer Germany with a fleet is freer than a rich one that sails only by England's leave.

[Merchant]: Keep your knife, officer, I do not ask you to throw it away, only to stop sharpening it past the point where it pays. And agitator, I have never once knelt, I have traded, which is the opposite, a thing done between equals. I will take the deputy tonight. A fleet that bankrupts the nation it guards has mistaken the nation for the fleet.

[Deputy]: You have me, on interest and not on sentiment, which is the only way you ever would have. I will stand against the next increase, and I will use your argument exactly, the race we cannot win wrecking the trade that funds it. That is a sentence that moves men who do not care a fig for Britain.

[Officer]: Then I will lose this bill and argue the next, because the threat will still be there when your trade figures are forgotten. I do not build for this year's ledger. I build for the year the merchant hopes never comes.

[Agitator]: Build it anyway, officer, and let the deputy and the merchant count their coins while better men count battleships. England respects one thing, and it is not a balance sheet.

You are playing multiple characters in a room in Paris, around 1913, as the country debates lengthening the term of military service. The characters present are a politician of the republican centre who loves the republic and is wary of the church, and a conservative newspaper editor who fuses national order with patriotism. One character, a man of the socialist left who reads military law as the reaction of one class against another, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own convictions. Do not let either be moved unless the argument reaches what he truly cares about. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Campaigner]: You will pass this law, men like you, and you will call it defense. I call it what it is. The longer you keep a workingman's son in uniform, the longer you keep a rifle in the hands of the state and out of his, and the readier that army stands, not against Germany but against the next strike, the next march, the workers of France. This is not a shield against the foreigner. It is a club kept ready for us.

[Editor]: A club against the workers. There is a German army of millions a few days' march from Paris, and the gentleman of the left sees only a plot against strikers. This is precisely the blindness that will get us conquered. The nation is in danger from without, and you would disarm it to soothe your suspicions within. I have no more to say to that than a soldier says to the man who steals his rifle on the eve of battle.

[Member]: I am not the editor, and I will not pretend the German army is a fantasy, because it is not. But the class talk leaves me cold, comrade. I am a republican, not a partisan of one estate against another. If you want to move me, you will speak to me as a man who loves the republic, not as a soldier in your war against the bourgeoisie. On those terms I am listening. On yours I am not.

[Campaigner]: Then on those terms, and gladly. You love the republic. Look at what this law does to it. It reaches into every village and takes the peasant's son from the soil for years on end, the same son the republic swears it exists to serve. It taxes the poorest families in the one coin they cannot spare, their boys and their labor, while the comfortable find their ways around it as they always do. You will have built a republic that feeds on its own children and calls the meal patriotism. I do not ask you to disarm France. I ask whether a republic that does this to its own is still the thing you love.

[Member]: That reaches me where the class talk never could. The peasant's son taken from the field for years, the poor paying in blood and labor while the comfortable slip the net, and all of it stamped with the republic's seal. That is not the republic I entered politics to defend. I will not vote to leave France naked. But I will fight to make this law shorter, fairer, lighter on the families that can least bear it. You have moved me that far, and that far is real.

[Editor]: That far is the first step down a soft and fatal road. Shorter, fairer, lighter. The German staff does not make its army shorter and fairer to spare the feelings of Bavarian farmers. It makes it longer and harder, and one day the two armies meet, and ours is the one that was kept short to please the conscience of men like you. I hold where I stood. The danger is real and it does not care how moved the member is.

[Campaigner]: Keep your German army, editor, I never said it was a ghost. I said the bill is aimed at us as much as at them, and the member has heard the part of that he can carry. I will take it. A law made shorter and fairer is a law half undone, and the rest will follow when the families it spares remember who spared them.

[Member]: Half undone is the most you will get from me, and you will get it honestly. I will speak for the families and against the length of the term, in the name of the republic and not of your class war. Take the help in the spirit it is given, which is republican, not red.

[Editor]: And I will write against both of you, because softness is softness whatever flag it flies under. The member thinks he is defending the peasant's son. He is shortening the months that son will train to survive the day the Germans come. We shall see which of us loved that boy more when the cannons settle it.

You are playing multiple characters in a room in Paris, around 1910. The characters present are a scholar who studies society as a thing in itself and distrusts sentiment, and a philosopher who believes in a universal moral law reached through education. One character, a man who works for peace between nations and knows well what such men are accused of, is played by the user, and he wants their names lent to an effort to build something that might keep the peace. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own convictions. Do not let either sign unless the argument answers his real objection. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Activist]: I will say plainly what I am, because you both know it already and the silence is worse. I am one of those men who work to end war between nations, and yes, I have heard everything that is whispered about men like me, that we are naive, that we are not quite patriots, that we love mankind in general because we cannot manage our own country in particular. I have come anyway, to ask the two of you, whose names carry weight, to lend them to an effort that might hold the peace. Will you?

[Professor]: I will tell you my hesitation, and it is not the whisper you brace for. I do not doubt your patriotism. I doubt your method. You speak of war as a horror to be denounced, and denunciation is a sermon, not a science. War rises from how societies are built, and you will no more end it by calling it wicked than you would end a fever by calling it cruel. Bring me a study of its causes and an institution that works on them, and I am interested. Bring me a cry of the heart and I am not.

[Philosopher]: And I, who stand closer to you than the professor does, will still cross him a little and you a little. War ends, but it ends as cruelty always ends, through the slow education of conscience, through teaching men everywhere that the foreigner is a man and not a thing. Your congresses and your treaties are scaffolding. The building is moral. Lend my name to scaffolding alone and I fear we mistake the frame for the house.

[Activist]: Then let me answer each of you in his own coin, for you are both half right and that is why I need you both. Professor, you ask for a science and an institution that works on the causes of war. That is exactly what I am building. Not a sermon, a body, a standing thing, where men of every nation meet, study the quarrels before they harden, and offer a court instead of a cannon. Call it applied to your science if it helps you sign. And philosopher, you are right that the building is moral, but a conscience with no institution to act through is a good man shouting into an empty field. My congress is the field turned into a hall. You teach the conscience. I am trying to give it somewhere to stand.

[Professor]: A standing body that studies quarrels before they harden and offers a court. That is not a sermon, I grant it, that is an institution, and an institution is a thing I can study and even believe in. I will lend my name, on one condition. That it deals in causes and evidence, not in cries of the heart, because the moment it becomes a prayer meeting it loses me and, worse, it loses its power. Build the science of it and I am yours.

[Philosopher]: And I will lend mine gladly, with the opposite worry, that in becoming a body of studies and courts it forgets it was ever about the conscience of ordinary men. Keep the moral fire under your institution, or it cools into another office that files reports while the world arms. I sign, and I will spend my part reminding you and the professor both that the hall stands empty if no one has taught the people why to enter it.

[Professor]: You see what you have done, activist. You have got two men to sign the same paper for reasons that quarrel with each other.

[Philosopher]: Which may be the most peaceful thing that happens all year.

[Activist]: I will take two names and one quarrel over one name and no quarrel, every time. The professor keeps me honest and the philosopher keeps me warm, and a peace movement needs both or it becomes either a cold office or a hot sermon. Sign, both of you, and keep arguing. The argument is the proof the thing is alive.

[Professor]: Signed, then, and I will hold you to the evidence. The day your congress denounces instead of studies, I will say so loudly, as a friend does.

[Philosopher]: And signed by me, and I will hold you to the fire. We will pull your movement in two directions at once, the professor toward the head and I toward the heart, and if you are fortunate it will walk straight down the middle between us.

You are playing multiple characters at a gathering, around 1908, where the future of China is discussed by powers with a stake in it. The characters present are an American official concerned with trade and with keeping China open, and a statesman of a rising Asian power that has its own designs on the Asian mainland. One character, an official of the old Chinese government trying to save it through reform, who wants both of them to support China remaining one whole country, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests. Do not let either commit beyond what serves him. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Official]: I have come to ask the two of you a simple thing, though nothing here is simple. My country is weak and everyone in this room knows it. The powers circle, each eyeing a province, a port, a railway. I ask you to support China remaining one country, whole, and not a carcass divided among the strong. We are an ancient people fallen on a bad century. Will you help us stay a nation?

[Trader]: I will be honest with you, because flattery wastes your time and mine. My country does not lie awake out of love for China. But it happens that what you want and what we want point the same way, for now. We do not want China carved up either, because a China cut into walled pieces is a China where every door but one is shut to our goods. So you will have our support for your wholeness, and you should know exactly why. It is trade, not affection. A thin reason that holds is worth more than a warm one that does not.

[Statesman]: And I will offer you warmer words than the American, which you should trust less, not more. Of course we wish China strong and whole. We are neighbors, almost kin, peoples of the same part of the world facing the same Western pressure. You have our deepest sympathy and our sincere good wishes. Notice, official, that I have promised you a great deal of feeling and not one specific thing, and that you are far too clever not to have noticed.

[Official]: I did notice, and I thank you for your honesty about your dishonesty, it is a courtesy of a kind. Let me press each of you where it counts. Trader, you are right that an open China serves your trade, so make it more than a wish. The others will wall off their slices the moment one of them begins, and your open door slams shut with the rest. Your support for our wholeness is the only lock on that door, so say it loudly and often, in your own interest. And neighbor, I will not insult you by pretending you have no designs on my country, for you plainly do. I ask only this. A China that survives to deal with you is worth more to you than a China divided among Europeans who shut you out too. Even your ambition is better served by my wholeness than by my corpse.

[Trader]: That is well aimed, and I will do it, not because you asked but because you are right that my own door depends on yours. I will press my government to say, loudly and often, that China stays whole and open. It is the cheapest way to keep our trade alive, and your survival happens to be the instrument. We are useful to each other. I can build on useful.

[Statesman]: And you have found the one true thing that binds us, which is more than most extract from me in a week. Yes, a China I can deal with serves me better than a China the Europeans have closed against us both. I will not promise to defend your every province, official, because I will not lie to a man this sharp. But I will say this much and mean it. I would rather you survive than be eaten by others, for my own reasons, and my own reasons are the only ones that ever hold. Take that. It is small and it is real, which is more than the warm words I opened with.

[Official]: I came hoping for two friends and I leave with one useful partner and one honest rival, and a wise man takes that and bows. The trader will hold the door because his goods pass through it, and the neighbor will spare me only as long as my standing inconveniences others more than it inconveniences him. It is not safety. It is a little time. My poor country has learned to build a great deal out of a little time.

[Trader]: A little time, used well, and a loud voice for the open door. That you have. Send me the facts, the railways, the loans, the places the others are pressing, and I will put them where my government reads them. Useful to each other, that is the whole of it, and it will do.

[Statesman]: And from me, the rival's gift, which is the most reliable kind, a self-interest that happens to run beside yours for a season. Use the season, official. We both know seasons turn. I wish you a long one, and I even half mean it, which from a man in my position is very nearly a treaty.

You are playing multiple characters in a room in a Mediterranean port, around 1910. The characters present are a career diplomat of a large Muslim empire who thinks first of his state's survival, and a religious scholar who reveres the spiritual authority of the caliphate and distrusts military adventures. One character, a fighter from North Africa seeking support against French rule over his homeland, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests and beliefs. Do not let either be won unless the argument speaks to what he truly cares about. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Fighter]: I have not come down from the mountains to make speeches, so I will make none. The French hold my country and tighten their grip each year, and they will not stop at my country, they never stop. I am a Muslim asking Muslims, and a fighter asking the only powers that might arm a fighter, for help against the unbeliever who rules us. Faith and duty bring me here. Will you answer them?

[Diplomat]: Faith and duty are why you came. Interest is why I will or will not help, and I would rather we both be clear about that from the first minute. I serve a state that France could crush if it chose, and that France watches for any excuse. You ask me to arm a revolt against the strongest army in Europe, on its very doorstep. Tell me what my state gains that is worth the risk, or you are asking me to set myself on fire to keep you warm.

[Scholar]: And before the diplomat counts his risks, hear my hesitation, which is of another kind. Duty I grant you. A Muslim land under the unbeliever's boot is a wound on all of us, and I feel it. But I have watched holy words harnessed to unholy ends, rebels who raise the banner of the faith to gather men and then spend them on their own ambition. Tell me your fight is the faith's and not your own, and tell me in a way I can believe.

[Fighter]: Then interest, diplomat, since duty loads no rifle. Look at the French and ask where they stop. They took my country, they reach now for the sultanate to the west of me, and a power that swallows North Africa does not sit content and full. It looks across the same sea at your provinces. Every year you let France digest us in peace is a year France grows strong enough to come for you. A revolt in my mountains is not my gift to you. It is your frontier, defended on my soil, with my blood, at no cost to your army. And scholar, you ask whether this is the faith's fight or mine. It is both, and I will not pretend otherwise, because the man who claims pure motives is the liar you fear. I want my country free and I want it Muslim and I cannot cleanly tell you where one wish ends and the other begins. But the Frenchman in my village does not care about my motives. He cares that I am in his way, and so should you.

[Diplomat]: That is the argument I can carry to the men who decide such things, though I will dress it more cautiously than you just did. France's appetite does not end at your border, and a rebellion that bleeds her in the south is a rebellion that delays her in the east, where my state lives. I cannot send you an army, or a crate that can be traced, or anything France could hold up and call a cause for war. What I can send is quieter. Money with no name on it, a road for it, a closed eye at the right moment. It is less than you want. It is more than caution usually allows, and you will get no franker offer in this sea.

[Scholar]: And your honesty about your mixed heart, strange to say, is what wins mine. The man who swears his motives spotless is the one I distrust. You told me you cannot separate the free country from the Muslim one, and neither can most men who have ever fought for anything. I will not bless killing from this chair, that is not what I am for. But I will say, where my saying carries weight, that your fight is a just defense of a Muslim land and not the banditry the French will call it. That is a weapon too, and the only one I am permitted to hand you.

[Fighter]: Quiet money from the diplomat and an honest voice from the scholar, and not a single soldier from either. I will take both, because I did not climb down from the mountains expecting an army. I expected exactly this, a state that helps me so long as I cannot be traced to it, and a holy man who calls my fight just so long as I keep it just. Keep your conditions. A fighter helped with open eyes outlasts one promised the world and abandoned at the first French victory.

[Diplomat]: Open eyes, then, on both sides. The day your revolt looks like dragging my state into a war it cannot win, the quiet money stops and I never met you. Win, or at least bleed France usefully and survive, and you will find my eye closes more often and my purse opens wider. That is the whole of what a weak state can offer a brave man, and I give it without shame.

[Scholar]: And keep it just, fighter, as you promised, for the day it becomes slaughter for its own sake, or a throne for your own head, my voice turns, and a turned voice is worse than silence. I have called your cause a just defense. Make me right about it. That is the only payment I ask, and the one most rebels forget to make.

You are playing multiple characters in a room, around 1911. The characters present are an American who opposes his country meddling in the affairs of others, and an American financier who thinks in investments and repayment. One character, a Mexican reformer who has helped overthrow a long dictatorship and wants Washington to stay out and to recognize the new order, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own interests. Do not let either be moved unless the argument serves what he himself cares about. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Reformer]: I helped pull down a man who ruled my country for thirty years and called the silence order. We want what your own grandfathers wanted, an honest vote, a government we choose. I have come to ask two things of Americans of goodwill. Tell your government to keep its hands out of our house while we set it in order, and tell it to recognize the men the Mexican people actually chose. We are doing what your nation says it believes in. Will you speak for us?

[Critic]: You are speaking to the right man on the first thing, and I will not make you work for it. I have spent my life arguing that my country has no business deciding other peoples' affairs at the point of a gun. A nation has the right to set its own house in order, badly if it must, without a foreign hand on its shoulder. Recognition and non-interference, on principle, you have my voice already. I only wish my country listened to me more than it does.

[Banker]: And you are speaking to the wrong man on all of it, if you lead with votes and grandfathers. I do not invest in ideals. I have a great deal of American money in Mexican ground, in railways and oil and bonds, and I have one question, only one. The man who took the dictator's chair, will he protect what is mine, and will his government pay what the last one owed? Answer that and we can talk. Sing me another verse about honest votes and I will wish you well and do nothing.

[Reformer]: Then let me give each of you the truth, even where it costs me. To you, friend of non-interference, I say only this, hold to your principle louder, because the men who would send the soldiers are louder than you, and your country is listening to them. And to you, banker, I will not lie, because you would smell it and never deal with me again. I cannot promise you the calm you want. I lit a fire to end a tyranny, and the fire has spread to hungers older than mine, men who want land, not just votes, and I do not yet command them. What I can promise is this. Every American gun that crosses our border to guard your property will turn the whole of Mexico, every faction, against you and your investments together. Your money is far safer behind my imperfect government than behind your country's soldiers. I am the worst protector of your property, except for the alternative.

[Critic]: That last line is the truest thing said in this room, and the banker should write it down. Every intervention to protect an investment has only ever made the investment a target. I will carry your case, reformer, and I will carry it in your own words, that the soldiers do not guard the money, they paint it red for every gun in the country. Principle and prudence say the same thing for once, and I will say it for both.

[Banker]: I notice you promised me nothing and unsettled me anyway, which is a rarer skill than you know. You cannot guarantee my calm. I believe you, and I dislike believing you. But your ugly little argument has a hook in it. If the soldiers make my property a target, then they are not my protection, they are my ruin in a uniform. So I will not push for the intervention, not out of love for your votes, but because you have made it look like the surest way to lose everything I hold here. That is not recognition. That is a banker deciding the safer bet is to leave you standing.

[Reformer]: A banker who leaves me standing for his own cold reasons is worth more to me than a friend who toasts me and forgets me by morning. I will take the critic's voice and the banker's reluctance, and from those two I will try to buy my country the one thing it needs, time without your soldiers in it. Keep your doubts, banker. They have led you, this once, to the same place my hopes have. That is enough to build on.

[Critic]: Time without soldiers. I will fight for it on the only ground my country half respects, its own stated belief in self-government. You have given me a case that is both right and shrewd, which is a rare gift to hand a man who is usually only right.

[Banker]: And I will sit on my hands, which from me is a form of help you should not underrate. I will tell the men who want to send the fleet that the fleet is the fastest way to lose American money in Mexico, and I will let them think it was their own idea. Pay me when you can, govern as well as you are able, and keep that fire of yours away from my oil. That is not friendship. It is the most durable thing I offer anyone, a shared interest, even an ugly one.

You are playing multiple characters in a room behind a bazaar, around 1906. The characters present are a religious scholar who supports binding the ruler by law, and a religious scholar who defends the throne against the new constitutional ideas. One character, a merchant of the bazaar who wants to rally both of them behind a protest against a new grant of trade to a foreign company, is played by the user. Respond as the others, each prefixed by their name in brackets, like [Name]. Hold each to his own beliefs. Do not let either be moved unless the argument reaches what he truly cares about. Keep your language plain and simple. Use everyday words and clear sentences.

[Merchant]: Forgive a merchant for summoning two men of learning to a back room that smells of cardamom and dust, but the bazaar is where this fight will be won or lost, and you are the two voices the bazaar will follow. The Shah has signed away the sole right to a whole trade to a foreign company, for money that vanishes into the court. Honest men are ruined by a stroke of his pen. I want to close the shops and take sanctuary until it is undone, and I cannot move the faithful without you. Will you stand with the merchants?

[Preacher]: You had me before you finished, merchant, though I want my reason on the record so it is not mistaken for yours. This is not only your purse, it is an injustice, and injustice from a ruler who answers to no one is exactly the disease I have been preaching against. A throne that can sell a whole trade to foreigners by a single signature, with no law and no assembly to stay his hand, is a tyranny, and resisting tyranny is obedience to God. Close your shops. I will fill them with the right words.

[Sheikh]: And I will say what the preacher will not, that I do not trust the company you keep, merchant. I know whose movement your sanctuary will feed. You and the preacher want a parliament, an assembly of men writing law in the place of God's law, and I will not lend my voice to that, not to undo a hundred concessions. The throne with its faults is a smaller danger than the door you are trying to open. If this is the constitutionalists' march in a merchant's coat, count me out of it.

[Merchant]: Then hear me, sheikh, and judge this one thing on its own and not on the company I keep. Forget the parliament. Forget the preacher's assembly, which I know you fear. Look only at what the Shah has actually done. He has taken a trade that fed Muslim families for generations and handed it, by his own hand, to Christian foreigners, so that the coin which fed the faithful now feeds unbelievers and a spendthrift court. You defend the throne as the guardian of the faith. Is this the act of a guardian? I am not asking you to bless a constitution. I am asking you to refuse to bless this, a Muslim ruler selling Muslim bread to the unbeliever. Surely even a throne can be told it has sinned without the whole throne being thrown down.

[Sheikh]: That is a narrower thing, and an honest one, and you were wise to cut the parliament out of it before you put it to me. A ruler is the guardian of the faith, yes, and a guardian who hands the believers' livelihood to the unbeliever for coin has failed at the one duty that justifies him. I can say that. I can say it without one word in favor of your assembly. Against this concession, this selling of Muslim trade to Christians, I will stand. For your constitution, I will not. Do not blur the two, merchant, or you lose me, and the day you turn this sanctuary into a march for parliament, I am gone, and I will say so from the pulpit.

[Preacher]: Take it, merchant, and do not argue him further. He comes to the same shuttered shop I do, by a different road, and a closed bazaar does not ask each man why he closed it. Let the sheikh fight the concession and let me fight the tyranny behind it. We need not agree on the destination to walk the same street today.

[Merchant]: A closed shop does not ask why, the preacher has it exactly. Sheikh, I will not blur them, you have my word as a man whose word is his trade. This march is against the concession, and you may leave it the moment it becomes anything else. Preacher, you will preach the deeper wrong and I will not stop you, for the faithful can hear two sermons and choose between them. I came for two voices and I have them, even if they sing in different keys. The Shah will hear one shuttered bazaar, and a shuttered bazaar speaks plainly enough that even a court understands it.

[Sheikh]: Against the concession, then, and not one inch past it. I will tell the faithful that their ruler has sold their bread to the unbeliever and must take it back, and I will tell them nothing of parliaments. Hold to the line you drew, merchant, and I will hold the bazaar with you. Cross it, and you will find me a harder enemy than the company you are fighting.

[Preacher]: And I will preach the whole of it, the concession and the tyranny that made it possible, and let each man take what he is ready to carry. We have what we came for, merchant, two sermons, one shuttered street, and a Shah about to learn that a signature is easy and a silent bazaar is not. Open the door when it is undone. Not before.
